narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 71% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged primarily on three key factors from your profile: your LSAT score, your Year 3 school decile ranking, and your Year 1 school decile ranking. The most influential was your LSAT score of 25, well below the typical 36.7 average, which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure by signaling lower readiness compared to students who succeed. Next, your Year 3 decile of 3—lower than the average 5.53—further reinforced concerns about your later academic performance relative to peers. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 5, close to the average of 5.71, worked in your favor by suggesting stronger early potential and slightly pulling the prediction toward passing. Other details like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status played lesser roles, as full-time is the norm for over 90% of students. Overall, your profile stood out as riskier than typical admitted students, whose stronger test scores and consistent rankings align with bar passage. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 also trails the 3.21 average, it wasn't the top driver here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male pursuing law—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes patterns from historical data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your grades in later years, and reapplying with those improvements to shift future predictions. You might also explore targeted bar prep programs or schools with more flexible admissions to build your case. With focused effort, you can rewrite this story.
